title: Chutes and Ladders: Dynamical Automorphisms via the ZX-Calculus
authors: Alexander Frei, Sascha Zakaib-Bernier, Zachary Mann, Michael Vasmer, Victor V. Albert
abstract: The ZX-calculus is a powerful graphical language for manipulating quantum circuits, which has recently found many applications in quantum error correction. We extend this language to handle Floquet and other dynamical stabilizer codes via the connection between measurement-based code switching and gauge fixing (arXiv:1810.10037). We combine gauge-fixing steps to implement a closed loop in the space of stabilizer codes, returning to the original codespace up to a logical Clifford gate. These measurement-based paths in the space of stabilizer codes can be viewed as shortcuts, or "chutes and ladders", relative to single-qubit Clifford operations and qubit permutations. This yields a machine-interpretable method for constructing dynamical automorphisms and facilitates the search for implementations of desired logical gates. As an example, we implement a logical phase gate via distance-preserving code switching for the seven-qubit code bare code (arXiv:1702.01155), which has no non-trivial logical Clifford gates based on single-qubit Clifford operations and qubit permutations (arXiv:2409.18175).
summary: This paper introduces a powerful graphical method using the ZX-calculus to study dynamical automorphisms in quantum error correction codes. By modeling code switching as a path in the space of stabilizer codes, the authors provide a systematic way to construct these automorphisms. This machinery allows for the theoretical design and search for implementations of complex logical quantum gates.
main_problem: To develop a machine-interpretable method for constructing dynamical automorphisms for quantum error correction (QEC) codes, particularly for handling dynamical stabilizer codes.
main_result: The authors show that dynamical automorphisms can be constructed by viewing code switching as a closed loop in the space of stabilizer codes, which they term 'chutes and ladders' relative to local Clifford operations.
method: The core method extends the ZX-calculus to model gauge fixing, which is used to implement these paths in the space of stabilizer codes by combining gauge-fixing steps.
model_or_system: The work focuses on stabilizer codes, represented by their ZX-encoding graphs, and extends this framework to subsystem codes and dynamical codes. The mathematical tools involve the ZX-calculus, graph theory, and symplectic representations.
key_observables: Dynamical automorphisms, logical gates (e.g., logical S or phase gate), and the structure of the Local Clifford (LC) orbit graph.
important_parameters: The code parameters $[[n, k, d]]$ (e.g., the 7-qubit code), and the local Clifford group elements.
main_assumptions: The method relies on the connection between measurement-based code-switching and gauge fixing, and the ability to define a unique normal form for ZX-encoding graphs.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate ZX-encoding graphs, showing how gauge fixing creates closed loops (dynamical automorphisms) in the space of stabilizer codes, and demonstrating the implementation of specific logical gates.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops the ZX-calculus formalism for subsystem codes, establishes a unique normal form for these graphs, and then applies this framework to show that gauge fixing generates dynamical automorphisms that can implement desired logical gates.
why_interesting: The rigorous mathematical framework connecting gauge fixing, graph theory, and code structure provides powerful tools for understanding the underlying symmetries and computational capabilities of quantum error-correcting codes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. ZX-encoding graphs of subsystem codes that gauge fix into a stabilizer code. From left to right, we have the ZX- encoding graph of a stabilizer code and, in brackets, the subsystem codes that Z/X/Y gauge fix into the stabilizer code.

FIG. 2. Dynamical automorphism of the [[4, 2, 2]] code. We extend the original ZX-encoding graph (a) to construct a subsystem code (b) that Y gauge-fixes into the [[4, 2, 2]] code up to some local Clifford gates and a logical CZ gate (highlighted in gray).

FIG. 4. Local Clifford (LC) orbit of the 3-qubit code from Fig. 3a, whose ZX-encoding graph is on the far right side of the diagram. Here, the equalities between ZX-encoding graphs refer to LC equivalence, whereas the looping arrows represent LC gates that bring the graph to itself. The gates above them keep track of the logical gate induced by the given LC, and we do not keep track of the physical gates they in- duce. The red arrows correspond to the X and Y gauge fixings seen in Fig. 3b & Fig. 3c, respectively.

FIG. 3. Gauge fixing examples and dynamical automorphism for a 3-qubit code. (a) ZX-encoding graph of this code. (b) X gauge fixing of a subsystem code built by extending (a), giving two equivalent ZX-encoding graphs. (c) Dynamical automorphism generating a logical S gate consisting of a Y gauge fixing, local Clifford gates, and a qubit permutation.

FIG. 5. Dynamical automorphism for the bare code implementing a logical S gate: (a) Closed loop in the space of [[7, 1, 3]] stabilizer codes. The blue squares are subsystem codes that Z/X/Y gauge fix into stabilizer codes (orange bubbles). See [64, Figure 12] for more details. (b) ZX-encoding graph of the [[7, 1, 3]] bare code [68]. We label the logical qubit wire ℓand the physical qubit wires 1, . . . , 7. (c) Y -gauge fixing of subsystem A1 from the bare code (108) into stabilizer code (257). (d) Y -gauge fixing of C1 from (257) into (185). (e) Y -gauge fixing of D1 from (185) into (240). (f) X-gauge fixing of A1 from (240) back into the bare code (up to local Cliffords and qubit...
